[[Marley Leuluai]] (cousin) }} }} '''Thomas James Leuluai''' (born 22 June 1985) is a New Zealand professional [[rugby league]] coach who is an assistant coach at the [[Wigan Warriors]] in the [[Super League]] and a former professional rugby league footballer who played for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] at international level. He played for the [[New Zealand Warriors]] in the [[National Rugby League|NRL]] in two separate spells in [[Auckland]], the [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]] in the [[Super League]], before moving to [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] for the first of his two spells at the club. Leuluai was a member of the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup|2008 World Cup]]-winning [[New Zealand]] team and has also played for the [[Exiles rugby league team|Exiles]]. ==Background== Leuluai was born in [[Auckland, New Zealand]], and is of [[Samoa]]n and [[Māori people|Māori]] descent. Leuluai's father is former Maori representative/New Zealand international [[James Leuluai]], who scored two tries for [[Hull F.C.]] in the [[1985 Challenge Cup]] Final 6 weeks before Thomas was born. Thomas was educated at [[Mount Albert Grammar School]] where he attended school with fellow rugby league players [[Sonny Bill Williams]], [[Steve Matai]] and [[Tevita Latu]]. Leuluai's brother [[Macgraff Leuluai]] currently plays for [[Widnes Vikings]], his uncle [[Phillip Leuluai]] played for [[Salford Red Devils|Salford City Reds]] and his cousin [[Kylie Leuluai]] played for the [[Leeds Rhinos]].{{citation needed|date=October 2022}} ==Playing career== ===New Zealand Warriors=== Leuluai started his professional career at [[National Rugby League]] side [[New Zealand Warriors]] in 2003. He also played for the [[Junior Kiwis]]. At the time of his début he was the youngest player to play for the club, coincidentally in the same game [[Mark Darren Robinson|Mark Robinson]] became the oldest player to make his début for the club. With competition from New Zealand internationals [[Stacey Jones]] and [[Lance Hohaia]] he had limited first team experience at New Zealand Warriors and consequently he spent most of the 2003 season in the [[Bartercard Cup]] playing for his youth club [[Otahuhu Leopards|Otahuhu-Ellerslie]], playing 10 games and scoring 5 tries. Leuluai became the second youngest player ever to represent the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|Kiwis]] when he made his début that year.{{citation needed|date=October 2022}} [[File:Thomas Leuluai 2004 Warriors.jpg|thumb|right|150px|Leuluai playing for the Warriors at the SFS]] At the end of 2004, he left the Warriors after playing 21 first grade games and scoring two tries. ===London Broncos=== In 2004, Leuluai signed a contract with Super League side [[London Broncos#1994–2005: Broncos and Super League|London Broncos]] as a replacement for [[Dennis Moran (rugby league)|Dennis Moran]] who had left the Broncos to join the [[Wigan Warriors]]. He spent two years at the club playing 38 games and scoring 19 tries. Both seasons were affected by injury. In 2005, his season was cut short by a broken ankle{{cite news | last = Rae | first = Richard | title = London calling | work = From The Sunday Times | publisher = Times Newspapers Ltd. | date = 5 February 2006 | url = http://www.timesonline.co.uk/tol/sport/article726890.ece | access-date = 27 December 2009 | archive-date = 29 April 2024 | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20240429124107/https://www.thetimes.co.uk/ | url-status = dead }} while his 2006 campaign was disrupted by a [[hamstring]] injury sustained while playing for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]]. His contract was not extended and he agreed to join the [[Wigan Warriors]] for the 2007 season. ===Wigan Warriors=== Leuluai agreed a three-year deal with Wigan Warriors in December 2006 after months of speculation and rumours. He would replace Australians [[Michael Dobson (rugby)|Michael Dobson]] and partner [[Trent Barrett]] in the halves during the 2007 season. Leuluai made his Wigan Super League début in a 16–10 defeat by the [[Warrington Wolves]] at the [[JJB Stadium]] on 9 February 2007. He scored the game-winning try against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] at the JJB stadium and scored Wigan's first against the [[Bradford Bulls]] in the history breaking 31–30 comeback win in the [[Super League#Structure|Play-offs]].{{citation needed|date=October 2022}} [[File:Tommy Leuliai.jpg|thumb|right|Leuluai playing for the Wigan Warriors in 2008]] On 9 May 2008 Leuluai played for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] at the Sydney Cricket Ground in the Centenary Test, which took place 100 years to the day after the New Zealand 'All Golds' first met Australia. [[Image:Thomas Leuluai (26 October 2008).jpg|thumb|right|Tommy Leuluai playing for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|Kiwis]] at the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup]].]] At the end of 2008 Leuluai played for the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup squads#New Zealand|New Zealand]] team which won the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup|2008 World Cup]].{{cite web|url=http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_3949034,00.html|title=Kiwis select Sonny Bill|publisher=Sky Sports|date=8 August 2008|access-date=8 August 2008|archive-date=24 September 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150924151633/http://www.skysports.com/story/0,19528,12196_3949034,00.html|url-status=live}}{{cite web|url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7656238.stm|title=Kiwis to wait on Webb and Matai|publisher=BBC|date=7 October 2008|access-date=7 October 2008|archive-date=10 October 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20081010164051/http://news.bbc.co.uk/sport2/hi/rugby_league/7656238.stm|url-status=live}} In April 2009, having made over seventy appearances for the club, Leuluai extended his contract with the Wigan Warriors by three years to last him until the 2012 season.{{cite web|url=http://www.wigantoday.net/warriors/Leuluai-pens-new-deal.5196124.jp|title=Leuluai pens new deal|publisher=www.wigantoday.net|access-date=22 April 2009}} He helped Wigan to their first Championship since 1998 with a man-of-the-match performance in the [[2010 Super League Grand Final]] earning him the [[Harry Sunderland Trophy]] in the victory over [[St Helens R.F.C.|St. Helens]] at [[Old Trafford]].{{Cite news | last = Hadfield | first = Dave | title = Maguire proves the over the [[Leeds Rhinos]], scoring a try at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite news|last=Scott|first=Ged|title=Leeds 18-28 Wigan|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/14670204|access-date=21 November archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131005080904/http://www.theguardian.com/sport/2011/aug/27/leeds-rhinos-wigan-warriors-challenge-cup | url-status=live }} ===Return to New Zealand=== In November 2011 Leuluai announced that he had signed a three-year contract with the [[New Zealand Warriors]] starting in 2013.[http://www.stuff.co.nz/sport/league/6000013/Thomas-Leuluai-signs-with-Warriors-for-2013 Thomas Leuluai signs with Warriors for 2013] {{Webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111121040635/http://www.stuff.co.nz/sport/league/6000013/Thomas-Leuluai-signs-with-Warriors-for-2013 |date=21 November 2011 }} ''stuff.co.nz'', 20 November 2011 [[File:Thomas Leuluai.jpg|thumb|right|Leuluai playing for Warriors in 2013]] In 2016 he was linked with a move back to [[Super League]] with [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]],{{cite web|url=http://www.loverugbyleague.com/news_21596-leuluai-linked-with-st-helens.html|title=Leuluai linked with St Helens - Love Rugby League|date=14 April 2016|access-date=19 April 2016|archive-date=18 April 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160418201919/http://www.loverugbyleague.com/news_21596-leuluai-linked-with-st-helens.html|url-status=live}} However, in July 2016, Leuluai announced he would be re-joining the [[Wigan Warriors]] in 2017.{{Cite web|url=https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors/warriors-lead-the-race-for-leuluai-1-7942439|title=Warriors lead the race for Leuluai|website=www.wigantoday.net|language=en|access-date=2018-01-23|archive-date=24 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180124070914/https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors/warriors-lead-the-race-for-leuluai-1-7942439|url-status=live}} === Wigan comeback === Having been awarded the number 7 jersey on his return to [[Wigan Warriors|Wigan]] he made his second début against [[Salford Red Devils]] in Round 1 of the 2017 [[Super League]] season. A week later he helped Wigan to lift the [[World Club Challenge]] against the [[Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks]] scoring his first try of the season a week later against [[Widnes Vikings]]. Leuluai suffered a broken jaw in Round 11 of the Super League just six months after suffering the same injury playing for [[New Zealand Rugby League|New Zealand]].{{Cite web|url=https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors/injury-blow-for-leuluai-1-8509590|title=Injury blow for Leuluai|website=www.wigantoday.net|language=en|access-date=2018-01-23|archive-date=24 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180124070718/https://www.wigantoday.net/sport/rugby-league/wigan-warriors/injury-blow-for-leuluai-1-8509590|url-status=live}} He made his comeback just four weeks later against [[St Helens R.F.C.|St Helens]], scoring his second try of the season against [[Wakefield Trinity]]. He played in the [[2017 Challenge Cup Final]] defeat by [[Hull F.C.]] at [[Wembley Stadium]].{{cite web|url=https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/challenge-cup-final-live-score-11054626|title=Hull FC 18-14 Wigan recap as the Black and Whites claim back-to-back Challenge Cup triumphs|publisher=Mirror|date=26 August 2017|access-date=6 November 2019|archive-date=6 November 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191106125620/https://www.mirror.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/challenge-cup-final-live-score-11054626|url-status=live}} He scored his third and final try of the season against [[Castleford Tigers]] in the sixth round of the Super 8's. His good form saw his called up to the New Zealand [[Rugby League World Cup|World Cup]] squad playing twice for the Kiwis. After telling coach [[Shaun Wane]] he felt more comfortable in the {{rlp|hk}} role he was handed the number 9 shirt after the departure of [[Michael McIlorum]].{{Cite web|url=http://wiganwarriors.com/news/2018-01-16-2018-squad-numbers|title=2018 Squad Numbers {{!}} News {{!}} Wigan Warriors|website=wiganwarriors.com|access-date=2018-01-23|archive-date=24 January 2018|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180124135623/http://wiganwarriors.com/news/2018-01-16-2018-squad-numbers|url-status=live}} He played in the [[2018 Super League Grand Final]] victory over the [[Warrington Wolves]] at [[Old Trafford]].{{cite web|url=https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2018/oct/13/super-league-grand-final-2018-wigan-warriors-v-warrington-wolves-live?page=with:block-5bc1ff43e4b05798be94cc63#liveblog-navigation|title=Super League Grand Final 2018: Wigan 12-4 Warrington – as it happened|publisher=Guardian|date=13 October 2018|access-date=5 November 2019|archive-date=17 December 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20191217180144/https://www.theguardian.com/sport/live/2018/oct/13/super-league-grand-final-2018-wigan-warriors-v-warrington-wolves-live?page=with:block-5bc1ff43e4b05798be94cc63#liveblog-navigation|url-status=live}} He played in the [[2020 Super League Grand Final]] which Wigan lost 8-4 against St Helens.{{cite news|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55072997|title=Super League Grand Final: Wigan 4-8 St Helens|work=BBC Sport|access-date=29 November 2020|archive-date=30 November 2020|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201130003606/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/55072997|url-status=live}} On 28 May, Leuluai played for Wigan in their [[2022 Challenge Cup Final]] win over [[Huddersfield Giants|Huddersfield]].{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/61594770.amp/|title=Challenge Cup final: Huddersfield Giants 14-16 Wigan Warriors|website=www.bbc.co.uk|date=28 May 2022|access-date=29 May 2022|archive-date=29 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220529051410/https://www.bbc.com/sport/rugby-league/61594770.amp|url-status=live}} ===Retirement and Wigan assistant coach=== On 1 September 2022, it was announced that Leuluai would be retiring at the end of the 2022 season, to replace [[Lee Briers]] as the assistant manager. Leuluai also announced that he will lead out [[New Zealand Rugby League|New Zealand]] in a [[2021 Rugby League World Cup|World Cup]] warm up match, against [[Leeds Rhinos]] on 8 October 2022, before joining the coaching staff at [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]].{{cite web|url=https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/62743023|title=Wigan Warriors legend to retire at end of season & join coaching staff|website=www.bbc.co.uk|date=1 September 2022|access-date=2 September 2022|archive-date=2 September 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220902072405/https://www.bbc.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/62743023|url-status=live}} In 2023, Leuluai came out of retirement to play for Wigan RU with fellow assistant coach [[Sean O'Loughlin]], scoring on his debut.{{cite web | url=https://www.examinerlive.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/watch-tommy-leuluai-union-try-26160929.amp | title=Watch Tommy Leuluai score superb individual try in rugby union debut | date=5 February 2023 | access-date=5 February 2023 | archive-date=5 February 2023 | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230205130922/https://www.examinerlive.co.uk/sport/rugby-league/watch-tommy-leuluai-union-try-26160929.amp | url-status=live }} ==Career statistics== {{cite web|title=Thomas Leuluai|url=https://www.wiganwarriors.com/teams/first-team/thomas-leuluai|publisher=Wigan Warriors|access-date=27 January 2019|archive-date=27 January 2019|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20190127205715/https://www.wiganwarriors.com/teams/first-team/thomas-leuluai|url-status=live}} {|class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" |- style="background:#bdb76b;" !
